lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: What are they that would speak with me?

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Sailors, sir: they say they have letters for you.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Let them come in. I do not know from what part of

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: the world I should be greeted, if not from Lord Hamlet.

lines.speaker: FIRST SAILOR
lines.text: God bless you, sir.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Let him bless thee too.

lines.speaker: FIRST SAILOR
lines.text: He shall, sir, an’t please him. There’s a letter for you, sir;

lines.speaker: FIRST SAILOR
lines.text: it comes from the ambassador that was bound for England; if your

lines.speaker: FIRST SAILOR
lines.text: name be Horatio, as I am let to know it is.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: “Horatio, when thou shalt have overlooked this, give these fellows some means

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: to the king: they have letters for him. Ere we were two

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: days old at sea, a pirate of very warlike appointment gave us

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: chase. Finding ourselves too slow of sail, we put on a compelled

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: valour, and in the grapple I boarded them: on the instant they

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: got clear of our ship; so I alone became their prisoner. They

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: have dealt with me like thieves of mercy: but they knew what

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: they did; I am to do a good turn for them. Let

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: the king have the letters I have sent; and repair thou to

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: me with as much speed as thou wouldst fly death. I have

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: words to speak in thine ear will make thee dumb; yet are

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: they much too light for the bore of the matter. These good

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: fellows will bring thee where I am. Rosencrantz and Guildenstern hold their

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: course for England: of them I have much to tell thee. Farewell.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Come, I will make you way for these your letters; And do’t

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: the speedier, that you may direct me To him from whom you

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: brought them.

annotations.title: Hamlet's letter and the pirate attack
annotations.body: Hamlet's letter to Horatio, delivered by sailors, is the play's most improbable plot device: pirates attack the ship, Hamlet boards them in the fight, and they return him to Denmark. Shakespeare adapted this from Belleforest's Histoires Tragiques, where a similar escape occurs. The randomness is essential — the providence Hamlet will later credit ('There's a divinity that shapes our ends') requires a genuinely unscripted event. Critics have noted the structural parallel to the shipwreck in Twelfth Night and the maritime accidents that reshape The Tempest. In Hamlet, the ocean becomes the place where the prince's fate escapes Claudius's careful management and returns to him transformed.

annotations.title: 'Thieves of mercy'
annotations.body: Hamlet's oxymoron — 'thieves of mercy' — is as compact a piece of rhetoric as anything in the play. Pirates who spared his life and set him back on his own quest have behaved better than the Christian courtiers who sent him to die. The paradox preserves the play's moral disorientation: an honorable thief, a loyal pirate, a treacherous king. The short scene is a technical marvel — four speeches of report that move the plot from England to Denmark without a single on-stage action. Shakespeare trusts the audience to register the transition on the strength of letter-reading alone.

trials.prompt: How did Hamlet escape the voyage to England?
options: A storm forced the ship back to Denmark.
He swam ashore when the ship anchored near the coast.
Pirates attacked the ship; Hamlet boarded their vessel in the fight and was returned to Denmark.
He bribed Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to turn the ship around.

trials.prompt: What does Hamlet's phrase 'thieves of mercy' express?
options: Thieves of pity who stole his valuables.
The pirates who spared his life and returned him home — honorable behavior from dishonorable men.
Merciful thieves who work only by night.
A metaphor for Claudius and his courtiers, who steal justice from the kingdom.

trials.prompt: What does Hamlet ask Horatio to do in the letter?
options: Bring his weapons and armor immediately.
Deliver a letter to the King and bring him to Hamlet's location.
Flee to Wittenberg and await further instructions.
Tell Ophelia that Hamlet is alive.
